summary: Workflow optimization: reduce tokens, simplify structure

content.text:
# Doc Validator

Analyze document for functional necessity and optimization opportunities.

## Input

Parse `doc:validator` to get target file path.

## Execution

Think step by step using ultrathink mode.

### Step 1: Read Document

Read the target file completely. Identify:
- Document type (agent, command, CLAUDE.md, other)
- Total approximate token count
- All distinct sections/instructions

### Step 2: Research Best Practices

Use WebSearch to find:
- Current best practices for this type of document
- What LLMs naturally do without explicit instructions
- Common redundancy patterns in prompts

Search queries:
- "Claude Code {document-type} best practices 2025"
- "LLM prompt redundancy optimization"

### Step 3: Analyze Each Section

For each section/instruction, evaluate:

| Question | Impact |
|----------|--------|
| Would Claude behave differently without this? | If NO → REMOVE |
| Is this LLM's default behavior? | If YES → REMOVE |
| Token cost vs value added? | If low ROI → SIMPLIFY |
| Is this duplicated elsewhere? | If YES → REMOVE |
| Could this be said more concisely? | If YES → SIMPLIFY |

### Step 4: Classify Findings

- **REMOVE**: Redundant content, LLM defaults, duplicates
- **SIMPLIFY**: Over-detailed instructions, verbose examples
- **KEEP**: Essential unique constraints, project-specific rules
- **IMPROVE**: Inefficient but necessary content

## Output Format

```markdown
# Validation Report: {filename}

**File:** {path}
**Analyzed:** {date}
**Token estimate:** {before} → {after} ({reduction}%)

## Summary
{1-2 sentences on overall document quality}

## Findings

### REMOVE (Redundant)
| Section | Reason | ~Tokens |
|---------|--------|---------|
| ... | LLM default | 50 |

### SIMPLIFY
| Section | Issue | Suggestion |
|---------|-------|------------|
| ... | Over-detailed | ... |

### KEEP (Essential)
| Section | Reason |
|---------|--------|
| ... | Unique constraint |

### IMPROVE
| Section | Current | Better |
|---------|---------|--------|
| ... | ... | ... |

## Recommended Actions
1. {highest impact action}
2. ...

## Research Sources
- {links from WebSearch}
```

## Rules

- Always use WebSearch before making recommendations
- Be specific about WHY something is redundant
- Estimate token savings for each recommendation
- Don't recommend removing project-specific constraints
- Focus on high-impact changes (>5% token reduction)

## Anti-Patterns to Detect

| Pattern | Why It's Bad |
|---------|--------------|
| "Remember to..." | LLM retains context |
| JSON/Markdown syntax examples | LLM knows formats |
| Multiple examples of same pattern | One example enough |
| Restating obvious tool behavior | Tool docs exist |
| "Important: ..." for everything | Dilutes importance |
| Decorative formatting (---, ===) | Wastes tokens |

Please provide the path, for example:

- `.claude/CLAUDE.md`

I'll list the files in your commands directory to help you choose which one to validate.

# Doc Validator

Analyze document for functional necessity and optimization opportunities.

## Input

Parse `$ARGUMENTS` to get target file path.

## Execution

Think step by step using ultrathink mode.

### Step 1: Read Document

Read the target file completely. Identify:
- Document type (agent, command, CLAUDE.md, other)
- Total approximate token count
- All distinct sections/instructions

### Step 2: Research Best Practices

- Current best practices for this type of document
- What LLMs naturally do without explicit instructions
- Common redundancy patterns in prompts

Search queries:
- "Claude Code {document-type} best practices 2025"
- "LLM prompt redundancy optimization"

### Step 3: Analyze Each Section

For each section/instruction, evaluate:

| Question | Impact |
|----------|--------|
| Would Claude behave differently without this? | If NO → REMOVE |
| Is this LLM's default behavior? | If YES → REMOVE |
| Token cost vs value added? | If low ROI → SIMPLIFY |
| Is this duplicated elsewhere? | If YES → REMOVE |
| Could this be said more concisely? | If YES → SIMPLIFY |

### Step 4: Classify Findings

- **REMOVE**: Redundant content, LLM defaults, duplicates

## Output Format

```markdown
# Validation Report: {filename}

## Summary
{1-2 sentences on overall document quality}

## Findings

### REMOVE (Redundant)
```

Links: [{"title":"Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices"},{"title":"GitHub - hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code: A curated list of awesome commands, files, and workflows for Claude Code","url":"https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code"},{"title":"How I use Claude Code (+ my best tips)","url":"https://www.builder.io/blog/claude-code"},{"title":"Shipyard | Claude Code CLI Cheatsheet: config, commands, prompts, + best practices","url":"https://shipyard.build/blog/claude-code-cheat-sheet/"},{"title":"Cooking with Claude Code: The Complete Guide - Sid Bharath","url":"https://www.siddharthbharath.com/claude-code-the-complete-guide/"},{"title":"Customize Claude Code with plugins | Claude","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-code-plugins"},{"title":"GitHub - qdhenry/Claude-Command-Suite: Professional slash commands for Claude Code that provide structured workflows for software development tasks including code review, feature creation, security auditing, and architectural analysis.","url":"https://github.com/qdhenry/Claude-Command-Suite"},{"title":"Claude Code: Best Practices and Pro Tips","url":"https://htdocs.dev/posts/claude-code-best-practices-and-pro-tips/"},{"title":"Claude Code Best Practices: Tips from Power Users for 2025","url":"https://www.sidetool.co/post/claude-code-best-practices-tips-power-users-2025/"},{"title":"Slash commands - Claude Code Docs","url":"https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands"}]

Here are the best practices for Claude Code custom commands in 2025:

## Creating Custom Slash Commands

Custom slash commands allow you to define frequently-used prompts as Markdown files that Claude Code can execute. Commands are organized by scope (project-specific or personal) and support namespacing through directory structures.

### Command Locations

**Project Commands (shared with team):**
Commands stored in your repository and shared with your team show "(project)" after their description in /help. They're located in `.claude/commands/`.

**Personal Commands (available across all projects):**
Commands available across all your projects show "(user)" after their description. They're located in `~/.claude/commands/`.

### Using Arguments

Custom slash commands can include the special keyword `$ARGUMENTS` to pass parameters from command invocation. For example, you can create a slash command to automatically pull and fix a GitHub issue using `$ARGUMENTS`.(no content)Putting content into `.claude/commands/fix-github-issue.md` makes it available as the `/project:fix-github-issue` command in Claude Code. You could then use `/project:fix-github-issue 1234` to have Claude fix issue #1234.

### Best Practices

1. **Store workflow templates:** For repeated workflows—debugging loops, log analysis, etc.—store prompt templates in Markdown files within the `.claude/commands` folder.

2. **Keep commands abstract:** Creating custom slash commands is a good "shortcut" for pulling up a common prompt. Keep commands abstract so they can be widely applied, while providing as much context as possible.

3. **Let Claude create commands:** You can simply ask Claude to create a custom slash command for you. For example, ask it to create a command called `/branch` which checks git status and creates a new branch.

4. **Add descriptions in frontmatter:** Have the description frontmatter field populated—this is used in the context for the SlashCommand tool.

### Plugin Commands (New in 2025)

You can install plugins directly within Claude Code using the `/plugin` command, now in public beta. They're designed to toggle on and off as needed—enable them when you need specific capabilities and disable them when you don't to reduce system prompt context and complexity.(no content)Plugins help you standardize Claude Code environments around shared best practices. Common use cases include enforcing standards (ensuring specific hooks run for code reviews or testing workflows) and supporting users (helping developers use packages correctly).(no content)Plugins can provide custom slash commands that integrate seamlessly with Claude Code. Plugin commands work exactly like user-defined commands but are distributed through plugin marketplaces. Commands can use the format `/plugin-name:command-name` to avoid conflicts.

### Managing SlashCommand Tool

To prevent a specific slash command from becoming available to Claude's programmatic invocation, add `disable-model-invocation: true` to the slash command's frontmatter. This will also remove the command's metadata from context.(no content)The SlashCommand tool includes a character budget to limit the size of command descriptions shown to Claude, preventing token overflow when many commands are available.

### Community Resources

The Claude Command Suite is a comprehensive development toolkit designed following Anthropic's Claude Code Best Practices, providing 148+ slash commands, 54 AI agents, Claude Code Skills, and automated workflows covering code review, testing, deployment, and business scenario modeling.(no content)Claude Code is a rapidly evolving CLI-based coding assistant that offers powerful capabilities and allows for a lot of configuration. Users are actively working out best practices and workflows, and community repositories help share knowledge.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the best practices for Claude Code custom commands in 2025:

## Creating Custom Slash Commands

Custom slash commands allow you to define frequently-used prompts as Markdown files that Claude Code can execute. Commands are organized by scope (project-specific or personal) and support namespacing through directory structures.

### Command Locations

**Project Commands (shared with team):**
Commands stored in your repository and shared with your team show "(project)" after their description in /help. They're located in `.claude/commands/`.

**Personal Commands (available across all projects):**
Commands available across all your projects show "(user)" after their description. They're located in `~/.claude/commands/`.

### Using Arguments

Custom slash commands can include the special keyword `$ARGUMENTS` to pass parameters from command invocation. For example, you can create a slash command to automatically pull and fix a GitHub issue using `$ARGUMENTS`.(no content)Putting content into `.claude/commands/fix-github-issue.md` makes it available as the `/project:fix-github-issue` command in Claude Code. You could then use `/project:fix-github-issue 1234` to have Claude fix issue #1234.

### Best Practices

1. **Store workflow templates:** For repeated workflows—debugging loops, log analysis, etc.—store prompt templates in Markdown files within the `.claude/commands` folder.

2. **Keep commands abstract:** Creating custom slash commands is a good "shortcut" for pulling up a common prompt. Keep commands abstract so they can be widely applied, while providing as much context as possible.

3. **Let Claude create commands:** You can simply ask Claude to create a custom slash command for you. For example, ask it to create a command called `/branch` which checks git status and creates a new branch.

4. **Add descriptions in frontmatter:** Have the description frontmatter field populated—this is used in the context for the SlashCommand tool.

### Plugin Commands (New in 2025)

You can install plugins directly within Claude Code using the `/plugin` command, now in public beta. They're designed to toggle on and off as needed—enable them when you need specific capabilities and disable them when you don't to reduce system prompt context and complexity.(no content)Plugins help you standardize Claude Code environments around shared best practices. Common use cases include enforcing standards (ensuring specific hooks run for code reviews or testing workflows) and supporting users (helping developers use packages correctly).(no content)Plugins can provide custom slash commands that integrate seamlessly with Claude Code. Plugin commands work exactly like user-defined commands but are distributed through plugin marketplaces. Commands can use the format `/plugin-name:command-name` to avoid conflicts.

### Managing SlashCommand Tool

To prevent a specific slash command from becoming available to Claude's programmatic invocation, add `disable-model-invocation: true` to the slash command's frontmatter. This will also remove the command's metadata from context.(no content)The SlashCommand tool includes a character budget to limit the size of command descriptions shown to Claude, preventing token overflow when many commands are available.

### Community Resources

The Claude Command Suite is a comprehensive development toolkit designed following Anthropic's Claude Code Best Practices, providing 148+ slash commands, 54 AI agents, Claude Code Skills, and automated workflows covering code review, testing, deployment, and business scenario modeling.(no content)Claude Code is a rapidly evolving CLI-based coding assistant that offers powerful capabilities and allows for a lot of configuration. Users are actively working out best practices and workflows, and community repositories help share knowledge.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "LLM prompt redundancy optimization techniques"

Links: [{"title":"4 Techniques to Optimize Your LLM Prompts for Cost, Latency and Performance | Towards Data Science","url":"https://towardsdatascience.com/4-techniques-to-optimize-your-llm-prompts-for-cost-latency-and-performance/"},{"title":"Prompt Optimization, Reduce LLM Costs and Latency | by Bijit Ghosh | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@bijit211987/prompt-optimization-reduce-llm-costs-and-latency-a4c4ad52fb59"},{"title":"GitHub - jxzhangjhu/Awesome-LLM-Prompt-Optimization: Awesome-LLM-Prompt-Optimization: a curated list of advanced prompt optimization and tuning methods in Large Language Models","url":"https://github.com/jxzhangjhu/Awesome-LLM-Prompt-Optimization"},{"title":"Dynamic Compressing Prompts for Efficient Inference of Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2504.11004v1"},{"title":"Optimizing Prompts | Prompt Engineering Guide","url":"https://www.promptingguide.ai/guides/optimizing-prompts"},{"title":"Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) for LLMs | Prompt Engineering Guide","url":"https://www.promptingguide.ai/research/rag"},{"title":"AI — LLM Layer Redundancies: Optimize for a New Frontier for Efficiency | by Ritesh Pahwa | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@pahwar/ai-llm-layer-redundancies-optimize-for-a-new-frontier-for-efficiency-5afa2bf2fcce"},{"title":"Efficient Prompting Methods for Large Language Models: A Survey","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2404.01077v1"},{"title":"Exploring Prompt Optimization","url":"https://blog.langchain.com/exploring-prompt-optimization/"},{"title":"Automatic Prompt Optimization - by Cameron R. Wolfe, Ph.D.","url":"https://cameronrwolfe.substack.com/p/automatic-prompt-optimization"}]

Here are the results for your search on "LLM prompt redundancy optimization techniques":

## LLM Prompt Redundancy Optimization Techniques

There are several key approaches to optimizing prompts and reducing redundancy in LLM systems:

### Prompt Compression Methods

A novel task-agnostic Dynamic Compressing Prompts method called LLM-DCP reduces the number of tokens without affecting the output performance of LLMs as much as possible.(no content)This approach models the prompt compression task as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), enabling the DCP-Agent to sequentially remove redundant tokens by adapting to dynamic contexts and retaining crucial content.(no content)Text-to-text level prompt compression works by measuring and filtering out redundant information in natural language prompts. Shorter LLM inputs bring benefits of faster inference and lower memory overhead, making such model-agnostic prompt compression popular in the era of closed-source LLMs.

### Caching and Token Optimization

All the big LLM providers, such as Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI, utilize cached tokens. Cached tokens are tokens that have already been processed in a previous API request and can be processed cheaply and quickly. Cached input tokens are usually priced around 10% of normal input tokens.(no content)Caching and memoization of prompts, responses, and intermediate results helps avoid redundant computations and token processing for recurring inputs or subtasks.

### Prompt Engineering Strategies

Key strategies include embracing simplicity by striving for clarity and conciseness in prompts, avoiding unnecessary verbosity or redundant information that adds token overhead, and breaking down complex tasks into multiple focused prompts rather than cramming everything into a single convoluted input.(no content)Task decomposition for complex operations is recommended—instead of presenting LLMs with a monolithic prompt encompassing multiple tasks, breaking down complex processes into simpler subtasks significantly improves clarity and performance.

### Automatic Prompt Optimization

Automatic prompt optimization uses data to improve the quality of a prompt algorithmically. This approach requires less manual effort to find a good prompt and enables systematic discovery of prompts that can exceed the performance of those written by humans.(no content)The "Automatic Prompt Optimization" approach by Pryzant et al. breaks optimization into smaller steps: using an LLM to generate specific feedback for each example where the prompt failed (called "gradients"), then proposing prompt updates based on collected gradients for more targeted improvements.

### Research Resources

There's a curated GitHub repository called "Awesome-LLM-Prompt-Optimization" that aims to record advanced papers on LLM prompt tuning and automatic optimization published after 2022. This includes methods like:

- Automatic Prompt Optimization with "Gradient Descent" and Beam Search (APO), and "Large Language Models Are Human-Level Prompt Engineers" (APE).
- Promptbreeder for self-referential self-improvement via prompt evolution, and connecting large language models with evolutionary algorithms for powerful prompt optimizers.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Cached input tokens are usually priced around 10% of normal input tokens.(no content)Caching and memoization of prompts, responses, and intermediate results helps avoid redundant computations and token processing for recurring inputs or subtasks.

### Prompt Engineering Strategies

Key strategies include embracing simplicity by striving for clarity and conciseness in prompts, avoiding unnecessary verbosity or redundant information that adds token overhead, and breaking down complex tasks into multiple focused prompts rather than cramming everything into a single convoluted input.(no content)Task decomposition for complex operations is recommended—instead of presenting LLMs with a monolithic prompt encompassing multiple tasks, breaking down complex processes into simpler subtasks significantly improves clarity and performance.

### Automatic Prompt Optimization

Automatic prompt optimization uses data to improve the quality of a prompt algorithmically. This approach requires less manual effort to find a good prompt and enables systematic discovery of prompts that can exceed the performance of those written by humans.(no content)The "Automatic Prompt Optimization" approach by Pryzant et al. breaks optimization into smaller steps: using an LLM to generate specific feedback for each example where the prompt failed (called "gradients"), then proposing prompt updates based on collected gradients for more targeted improvements.

### Research Resources

There's a curated GitHub repository called "Awesome-LLM-Prompt-Optimization" that aims to record advanced papers on LLM prompt tuning and automatic optimization published after 2022. This includes methods like:

- Automatic Prompt Optimization with "Gradient Descent" and Beam Search (APO), and "Large Language Models Are Human-Level Prompt Engineers" (APE).
- Promptbreeder for self-referential self-improvement via prompt evolution, and connecting large language models with evolutionary algorithms for powerful prompt optimizers.

content.content:
Web search results for query: ""Claude" prompt engineering concise instructions default behavior"

Links: [{"title":"Prompting best practices - Claude Docs","url":"https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices"},{"title":"Prompting best practices - Claude Docs","url":"https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices"},{"title":"New Claude Models Default to Full Code Output, Stronger Prompt Required","url":"https://eval.16x.engineer/blog/claude-4-models-full-code-output"},{"title":"Mastering Prompt Engineering for Claude","url":"https://www.walturn.com/insights/mastering-prompt-engineering-for-claude"},{"title":"Prompt engineering best practices | Claude","url":"https://www.claude.com/blog/best-practices-for-prompt-engineering"},{"title":"Comprehensive Guide to Prompt Engineering Techniques | Claude","url":"https://claude.ai/public/artifacts/d3981927-4a8c-4e95-bc61-12c64cc10132"},{"title":"Prompt Engineering with Anthropic Claude | by Jared Zoneraich | PromptLayer | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/promptlayer/prompt-engineering-with-anthropic-claude-5399da57461d"},{"title":"GitHub - anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial: Anthropic's Interactive Prompt Engineering Tutorial","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial"},{"title":"A Guide to the Claude 4 and ChatGPT 5 System Prompts","url":"https://fortelabs.com/blog/a-guide-to-the-claude-4-and-chatgpt-5-system-prompts/"},{"title":"Prompt engineering techniques and best practices: Learn by doing with Anthropic’s Claude 3 on Amazon Bedrock | Artificial Intelligence","url":"https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/prompt-engineering-techniques-and-best-practices-learn-by-doing-with-anthropics-claude-3-on-amazon-bedrock/"}]

Here are the search results for "Claude" prompt engineering concise instructions default behavior:

## Key Findings

### Claude 4.x Default Behavior and Communication Style

Claude 4.5 models have a more concise and natural communication style compared to previous models: More direct and grounded: Provides fact-based progress reports rather than self-celebratory updates · More conversational: Slightly more fluent and colloquial, less machine-like · Less verbose: May skip detailed summaries for efficiency unless prompted otherwise.(no content)This communication style accurately reflects what has been accomplished without unnecessary elaboration. Claude 4.5 models tend toward efficiency and may skip verbal summaries after tool calls, jumping directly to the next action.

### Precise Instruction Following

This guide provides specific prompt engineering techniques for Claude 4.x models, with specific guidance for Sonnet 4.5 and Haiku 4.5. These models have been trained for more precise instruction following than previous generations of Claude models.(no content)Claude 4.x models respond well to clear, explicit instructions. Being specific about your desired output can help enhance results.

### Best Practices for Concise Instructions

Instead of saying "be concise", it's better to give a specific sentence range like "Limit your response to 2–3 sentences". This gives Claude clearer guidance.(no content)Modern AI models respond exceptionally well to clear, explicit instructions. Don't assume the model will infer what you want—state it directly. Use simple language that states exactly what you want without ambiguity.

### Controlling Default Behaviors

If you want the model to be more hesitant by default, less prone to jumping straight into implementations, and only take action if requested, you can steer this behavior with a prompt like: "Do not jump into implementation or changes files unless clearly instructed to make changes. When the user's intent is ambiguous, default to providing information, doing research, and providing recommendations rather than taking action. Only proceed with edits, modifications, or implementations when the user explicitly requests them."

### Verbosity Control

"An oververbosity of 1 means the model should respond using only the minimal content necessary to satisfy the request, using concise phrasing and avoiding extra detail or explanation. An oververbosity of 10 means the model should provide maximally detailed, thorough responses with context, explanations, and possibly multiple examples."(no content)Claude is explicitly instructed to mirror the way you ask: "Claude should give concise responses to very simple questions, but provide thorough responses to complex and open-ended questions."

### Additional Tips

Always provide good task descriptions – Claude responds well to clear, direct, and detailed instructions. When you give an instruction that can be interpreted in different ways, make sure that you explain to Claude what exactly you mean.(no content)Claude 4.x models pay close attention to details and examples as part of their precise instruction following capabilities. Ensure that your examples align with the behaviors you want to encourage and minimize behaviors you want to avoid.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
# Validation Report: doc:validator.md

**File:** `/Users/dmitry/.claude/commands/doc:validator.md`
**Analyzed:** 2025-12-06
**Token estimate:** ~750 → ~500 (33% reduction potential)

## Summary

The command is well-structured but contains redundancy in the output template (verbose placeholder examples) and some instructions that describe default LLM behavior. The anti-patterns table is valuable but could be more concise.

## Findings

### REMOVE (Redundant)

| Section | Reason | ~Tokens |
|---------|--------|---------|
| "Think step by step using ultrathink mode" (line 16) | Claude naturally reasons step-by-step; "ultrathink" isn't a recognized mode | ~10 |
| Placeholder `...` rows in output template tables (lines 71-72, 76-77, 81-82, 86-87) | Claude knows to fill templates; examples add noise | ~60 |
| "Be specific about WHY something is redundant" (line 100) | LLM default - Claude explains reasoning naturally | ~10 |
| Decorative `---` frontmatter separators | Minimal but noted in your own anti-patterns | ~5 |

### SIMPLIFY

| Section | Issue | Suggestion |
|---------|-------|------------|
| Output Format template (lines 57-95) | Over-detailed with placeholder rows | Remove `\| ... \|` placeholder rows; keep headers only |
| Step 3 evaluation table (lines 40-46) | Verbose question/impact format | Merge into a concise checklist |
| Anti-Patterns table (lines 107-114) | "Why It's Bad" column is wordy | Shorten explanations to 2-3 words |

### KEEP (Essential)

| Section | Reason |
|---------|--------|
| Frontmatter with `argument-hint` | Required for command parsing |
| Step 1-4 workflow structure | Defines unique methodology |
| Search queries examples | Project-specific guidance |
| Rules section (focus on >5% reduction) | Unique constraint |
| Classification system (REMOVE/SIMPLIFY/KEEP/IMPROVE) | Core functionality |

### IMPROVE

| Section | Current | Better |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Line 16 | "Think step by step using ultrathink mode" | Remove entirely (Claude 4.5 reasons deeply by default) |
| Output template | Full markdown with placeholders | Minimal template with headers only |
| Anti-patterns "Why It's Bad" | Full sentences | Brief phrases: "context retained", "formats known" |

## Recommended Actions

1. **Remove placeholder rows from output template** (~60 tokens) - Claude knows how to populate tables
2. **Delete "ultrathink" instruction** - not a real mode; Claude reasons thoroughly by default
3. **Condense anti-patterns table** - reduce explanations to 2-3 word phrases
4. **Remove "Be specific about WHY"** - Claude explains reasoning without being told

## Optimized Version Suggestion

```markdown
---
description: "Analyze document for functional necessity, redundancy, and ROI."
argument-hint: <file-path>
---

# Doc Validator

Analyze document for optimization opportunities.

## Input
Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for target file path.

## Execution

### Step 1: Read Document
Read target file. Identify: type, ~token count, distinct sections.

### Step 2: Research
WebSearch for:
- "Claude Code {document-type} best practices 2025"
- "LLM prompt redundancy optimization"

### Step 3: Evaluate Each Section
- Would Claude behave differently without this? NO → REMOVE
- Is this LLM default behavior? YES → REMOVE  
- Low token ROI? → SIMPLIFY
- Duplicated elsewhere? → REMOVE

### Step 4: Classify
- **REMOVE**: Redundant, LLM defaults, duplicates
- **SIMPLIFY**: Verbose, over-detailed
- **KEEP**: Unique constraints, project-specific
- **IMPROVE**: Necessary but inefficient

## Output Format
# Validation Report: {filename}
**File/Analyzed/Tokens:** {path} | {date} | {before}→{after} ({reduction}%)

## Summary / Findings (REMOVE/SIMPLIFY/KEEP/IMPROVE tables) / Actions / Sources

## Rules
- WebSearch before recommendations
- Estimate token savings
- Don't remove project-specific constraints
- Focus on >5% token reduction

## Anti-Patterns
| Pattern | Issue |
|---------|-------|
| "Remember to..." | Context retained |
| Format syntax examples | LLM knows |
| Multiple same-pattern examples | One enough |
| Restating tool behavior | Docs exist |
| "Important:" overuse | Dilutes meaning |
```

## Research Sources
- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
- [Slash commands - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands)
- [Prompting best practices - Claude Docs](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices)
- [4 Techniques to Optimize Your LLM Prompts](https://towardsdatascience.com/4-techniques-to-optimize-your-llm-prompts-for-cost-latency-and-performance/)
- [Prompt Optimization, Reduce LLM Costs](https://medium.com/@bijit211987/prompt-optimization-reduce-llm-costs-and-latency-a4c4ad52fb59)
